content.text: The Thoughtworks Tech Radar Model

The Thoughtworks Technology Radar categorises technologies into four rings: ADOPT, TRIAL, ASSESS, and HOLD. Each ring encodes a confidence level and an implied investment signal for the organisation. ADOPT means the technology is ready for production use and teams should use it by default. TRIAL means the technology has enough evidence to justify a bounded pilot in a real project. ASSESS means the technology is worth tracking and may be worth a small spike or research investment. HOLD means the technology should not be started in new projects—existing uses may continue but should migrate.

For agentic AI, the radar is applied to four quadrants: LLM foundation models, orchestration frameworks, infrastructure components, and architectural patterns. The quadrant distinction matters because models and frameworks age differently: a model version may move from TRIAL to HOLD within six months as a newer version supersedes it, while an infrastructure pattern like vector-store indexing may remain in ADOPT for years.
Ring Definitions for Agentic AI

attrs.alt: Tech radar ring diagram showing ADOPT at centre, TRIAL, ASSESS, and HOLD as concentric rings with four quadrants for models, frameworks, infrastructure, and patterns
attrs.title: Agentic AI Tech Radar Ring Model

content.text: ADOPT

A technology earns ADOPT status when it has passed security review, has production runbooks written, has on-call support coverage, and has been validated by at least two independent product teams over a quarter. For LLM models, ADOPT additionally requires a passing score on the organisation's internal safety evaluation suite and a minimum uptime SLA from the provider. Engineers should default to ADOPT-ring technologies for new projects without needing Architecture Review Board sign-off.
TRIAL

TRIAL status applies to technologies that show strong promise but have not completed the full evaluation gauntlet. A TRIAL blip may be used in one bounded production workload (typically under 20% of traffic) with explicit Architecture Review Board approval, defined success criteria, and a review date no more than 90 days out. The team using the TRIAL technology must produce an experience report at the review date; a positive report triggers a TRIAL→ADOPT proposal.
ASSESS

ASSESS marks technologies that are worth monitoring but not yet ready for production investment. A blip in ASSESS should have a named owner in the architecture team who tracks its release notes, community activity, and security advisories. The owner presents a brief update at each quarterly radar review. ASSESS blips may be used in internal demos, spikes, and sandbox environments without production data.
HOLD

HOLD means stop new investment. Common reasons to HOLD an agentic AI technology include: unresolved security vulnerabilities, end-of-life or deprecation announcements, a failed TRIAL evaluation, or supersession by an ADOPT-ring alternative. Existing production deployments using a HOLD technology receive a migration deadline, typically six to twelve months for non-critical workloads and up to eighteen months for complex integrations.
Quadrants for an Agentic AI Radar
Models: foundation model versions (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.2), specialised fine-tuned variants, embedding models (text-embedding-3-large, BGE-M3)
Frameworks: orchestration libraries (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen), RAG frameworks (LlamaIndex, Haystack), evaluation toolkits (RAGAS, LangSmith)
Infrastructure: vector databases (pgvector, Weaviate, Pinecone), model gateways (LiteLLM, OpenRouter), observability platforms (Langfuse, Arize Phoenix)
Patterns: architectural patterns like ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, multi-agent orchestration, human-in-the-loop tiering, and context compression strategies
YAML Radar Entry Format

Each blip in the radar is expressed as a YAML entry with a unique identifier, ring assignment, a rationale paragraph written for a technical audience, and an optional caveats list. The changed flag marks when a blip moved rings since the previous edition, which the rendering tool uses to display movement arrows. Ring movement is always documented with an explicit previous_ring field so teams can trace the history.

# agentic-ai-radar.yaml
# Technology radar entries for the agentic AI platform.
# Maintained by the Architecture Review Board; updated quarterly.

radar:
  version: "2025-Q3"
  quadrants: [models, frameworks, infrastructure, patterns]
  rings: [ADOPT, TRIAL, ASSESS, HOLD]

blips:
  - id: BLP-001
    name: OpenAI GPT-4o
    quadrant: models
    ring: ADOPT
    changed: false
    rationale: >
      Production-proven for tool calling and structured output. Latency p99
      under 3s for 4k-token prompts. SOC 2 Type II certified. Strong function
      calling reliability vs alternatives in internal eval suite.
    caveats: [vendor lock-in, EU data residency requires Azure OpenAI endpoint]

  - id: BLP-002
    name: Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet
    quadrant: models
    ring: ADOPT
    changed: false
    rationale: >
      Best-in-class for long-context reasoning and structured document
      extraction. Preferred for compliance-sensitive workflows due to
      constitutional AI training reducing harmful output risk.
    caveats: [higher cost-per-token than GPT-4o for short prompts]

  - id: BLP-003
    name: LangGraph
    quadrant: frameworks
    ring: TRIAL
    changed: true
    previous_ring: ASSESS
    rationale: >
      Stateful agent orchestration with explicit graph definition. Enables
      reproducible checkpointing and human-in-the-loop pause points. Moving
      to TRIAL following successful PoC in two product squads.
    caveats: [learning curve for developers new to graph-based state machines]

  - id: BLP-004
    name: AutoGPT-style autonomous loops
    quadrant: patterns
    ring: HOLD
    changed: false
    rationale: >
      Unbounded recursive planning loops produce unpredictable tool call
      sequences and are incompatible with our cost governance policy.
      Use bounded orchestration patterns (max 10 hops) instead.
Python Ring Assignment Scorer

Ring assignments should be defensible, not subjective. The scorer below codifies the three evaluation dimensions—technical readiness, security and compliance, and operational maturity—into a weighted composite score that maps deterministically to a ring recommendation. Architecture Review Board discussions start from the scorer output and override it only with documented justification.
Evaluation Criteria by Ring Dimension
Technical readiness (40%): benchmark results on the organisation's eval suite, API stability (major version stable for 6+ months), latency p99 within SLA targets
Security and compliance (35%): SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certification, data residency options for relevant jurisdictions, vulnerability disclosure process, dependency supply chain audit
Operational maturity (25%): observability hooks available (traces, metrics, logs), production runbook written, on-call rotation covers the technology, capacity headroom documented
Radar Review Process

The radar review cadence is quarterly. Six weeks before the review date, the architecture team opens a blip nomination period: any engineer can propose a new blip or a ring movement for an existing one via a pull request to the radar YAML file. Three weeks before the review, nominated blips are triaged by a two-person architecture team: blips with insufficient evidence are deferred to the next quarter. One week before, the shortlisted blips are scored, and a draft radar is circulated to engineering leads for comment.
Week -6: open blip nomination via pull request; engineers add evidence in the blip rationale field
Week -3: triage session; architecture team defers blips with less than 30 days of production evidence
Week -1: scoring session; composite scores computed, ring recommendations produced, ARB review meeting held
Review day: radar published to internal portal; engineering leads receive a digest with all changed blips highlighted
Week +2: migration notices sent to teams using any newly-HOLD blips, with a migration timeline and recommended ADOPT-ring alternatives
Common Mistakes
Treating ASSESS as a holding pattern for technologies that will never move—ASSESS blips with no ring movement in four consecutive quarters should be archived
Placing model versions in ADOPT without version pinning—models under active development can change behaviour between patch releases, breaking evaluations
Publishing the radar without a rationale field—teams use the rationale to understand why a ring was assigned, and a missing rationale leads to shadow use of HOLD technologies
Not assigning a named owner to ASSESS blips—unowned blips go stale and are never promoted even when the technology matures
Best Practices
Keep blip rationales short—two to four sentences that answer why the ring was chosen and what would trigger a change
Pin model versions in ADOPT entries; separate entries for each major version so version-specific caveats are explicit
Automate the composite scorer as a CI step; score drift above a threshold triggers an automated JIRA ticket for an unscheduled review
Publish radar to an internal developer portal with search and filter; teams should be able to find the current status of any technology in under 30 seconds
Maintain a historical archive of past radar editions so teams can trace why a technology moved from TRIAL to HOLD, which is valuable for post-incident reviews
Decision Framework: Criteria by Ring

The following criteria are evaluated for each blip. Reviewers score each dimension from 0 to 1 and apply the weights to produce the composite score. A technology must meet ALL critical criteria (marked with asterisk) to qualify for ADOPT regardless of composite score.
Criterion
ADOPT
TRIAL
ASSESS
Security review*
Passed (SOC 2 or ISO 27001)
In progress or partial
Not yet started
Production evidence
2+ teams, 90+ days
1 bounded pilot
Sandbox or spike only
Runbook available*
Full runbook, on-call
Partial runbook
None required
API stability
Major version stable 6+ months
Beta or RC
Experimental or alpha
Radar Automation and Drift Monitoring

Between quarterly review sessions, the radar should not be a static document. A drift monitor checks each blip's composite score nightly—scores are refreshed from the evaluation pipeline—and alerts the blip owner if the current score would recommend a different ring than the assigned one. This surfaces ring-change candidates before the formal review session, preventing situations where a team continues using a blip that has silently degraded below its assigned ring threshold.

The drift monitor runs as a scheduled job in CI (nightly at 06:00 UTC). Drifted blips trigger a JIRA ticket assigned to the blip owner with the current score and recommended ring. Stale blips—those not reviewed within the cadence window—trigger an escalation to the architecture team lead. Both alert types are resolved either by re-scoring the blip (updating the evaluation pipeline outputs) or by formally moving the blip to the recommended ring at the next review session.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Thoughtworks Build Your Own Radar: open-source web app that renders a radar from a CSV or Google Sheet; good starting point for teams without custom infrastructure
AOE Tech Radar: React component library for rendering an interactive radar from JSON; easy to embed in an internal developer portal
Custom YAML + CI pipeline: most enterprise-scale teams outgrow spreadsheet-based radars; a YAML-in-source-control approach with the composite scorer in CI gives the most governance control

LLM version tracking: GitHub model release feeds and provider changelogs can be ingested by an automated pipeline that flags when a tracked model version receives a security advisory, triggering an out-of-cycle ASSESS review
Summary

The radar ring model—ADOPT, TRIAL, ASSESS, HOLD—gives engineering teams a shared vocabulary for technology investment decisions in agentic AI. Applying it to four quadrants (models, frameworks, infrastructure, patterns) captures the different lifecycle dynamics of each category. A weighted composite scorer makes ring assignments defensible, and a structured quarterly review cycle keeps the radar current. The key discipline is keeping each blip tightly scoped to a specific version or pattern, writing evidence-backed rationales, and enforcing migration deadlines for newly-HOLDed technologies rather than allowing indefinite shadow use.
